ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed bun class; no specific vegetable-filled pau entry exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers steamed bread and bun products; no vegetable-filled pau-specific code exists in the published hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for vegetable pau; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard vegetable pau piece weighs approximately 80 g and is typically consumed as a single snack or breakfast item.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Pau — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Vegetable Pau (CR ID F05020118). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Steamed bun (baozi) — analogous GI estimate based on Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable pau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest analogous food is steamed Chinese bun (baozi / mantou-style), which has a reported GI of approximately 45 (low range) in Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 29.7 × 0.80) / 100 = (45 × 23.76) / 100 ≈ 10.7. Vegetable filling and dietary fibre at 3.3 g/100g further moderate postprandial glucose response relative to plain steamed bun.

hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 365 mg/100 g is in the borderline-high range; individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one piece per meal and avoid additional high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or chilli sauce to keep total sodium within the recommended 2000 mg/day ceiling.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate 29.7 g/100 g, dietary fibre 3.3 g/100 g, estimated GI 45
type2_diabetes.guidance: With an estimated GI of 45 and dietary fibre of 3.3 g/100 g, vegetable pau produces a moderate glycaemic response; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit to one piece per meal and pair with non-starchy vegetables or a protein source to further blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 365 mg/100 g; saturated fat approximately 1.2 g/100 g; dietary fibre 3.3 g/100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The relatively low saturated fat (~1.2 g/100 g) and meaningful dietary fibre (3.3 g/100 g) are favourable for cardiovascular health, but the borderline sodium content (365 mg/100 g) warrants portion control to one piece per meal for individuals managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Low saturated fat, vegetable filling with antioxidants and beta-glucans, dietary fibre >3 g/100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The vegetable filling (cabbage, carrot, mushroom) provides antioxidants and beta-glucans with anti-inflammatory properties; saturated fat is low at approximately 1.2 g/100 g and trans fat is absent, making this a suitable choice for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when consumed without high-sodium dipping sauces.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable Pau (蔬菜包; Pau sayur) is a steamed Chinese bun filled with a savoury mixture of mixed vegetables — typically cabbage, carrot, and mushroom — seasoned with sesame oil and light soy sauce. The bun is made from a soft, leavened wheat-flour dough that is steamed until fluffy and slightly glossy. It is a staple breakfast and snack item in Singapore's hawker centres and coffee shops, widely available across Chinese, Malay, and Indian communities. Per 100 g it provides 195 kcal, 5.4 g protein, 6.1 g fat (saturated fat approximately 1.2 g), 29.7 g carbohydrate, 5.0 g sugar, 3.3 g dietary fibre, and 365 mg sodium. The dietary fibre content exceeds 3 g/100 g, which moderates postprandial glucose absorption. The estimated glycaemic index is 45 (low), making it a moderate-GI food suitable for most dietary patterns when portion-controlled. Clinically, the borderline sodium level (365 mg/100 g) requires attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The vegetable filling contributes antioxidants and beta-glucans relevant to anti-inflammatory dietary patterns. Vegetable pau is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-certified in most commercial preparations.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a vegetable pau?
Is vegetable pau suitable for diabetics?
Is vegetable pau high in sodium?
Can I eat vegetable pau if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of vegetable pau?
How much fibre is in vegetable pau?

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05020118) at confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2008 peer-reviewed source using the closest steamed Chinese bun equivalent; this is the best available approved evidence and is clearly documented.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for vegetable pau is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F05020118. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium is not reported in the HPB source for this entry. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond calcium and iron are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this food item as of 2026. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: steamed vegetable pau is a non-fried, plant-based product with no hydrogenated fats; trans fat is confirmed negligible. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: vegetable pau contains no animal-derived ingredients in standard formulation; cholesterol is confirmed zero. saturated_fat_g of 1.2 g/100 g is estimated from the total fat of 6.1 g/100 g using a typical vegetable-oil-based filling ratio consistent with HPB dietary guidance for similar products; HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry. GI value of 45 is an analogous estimate based on steamed Chinese bun (baozi) from Atkinson FS et al. (2008) International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values, Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283; no direct GI measurement for vegetable pau exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
